<h2>What You'll Find Here</h2>
A community-curated directory of top podcasts for software engineers, developer advocates, and tech founders. Whether you are improving enterprise architecture, checking out the latest frameworks, or seeking proven strategies in B2B SaaS and developer marketing, this collection links you to expert-led discussions throughout the product ecosystem.

---
  
  > **Notice:** Due to the massive number of podcasts in this directory, GitHub's web interface may truncate the bottom of this page. To view the complete list, please **[view the Raw file here](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Infrasity-Labs/awesome-developer-podcasts/main/README.md)** or **clone the repository** to your local machine.

| **C Programming for Everybody (cc4e.com)** | This is a podcast dedicated to learning the "classic" version of the C<br>Programming language from the 1978 book written by Brian W. Kernighan and Dennis<br>M. Ritchie. This book places the reader right in the middle of the 1970's<br>transition from a hardware-centered computer science to a focus on writing<br>portable and efficient software. C was used to develop operating systems like<br>Unix, Minix, and Linux. Programming languages like Python, Perl, Java,<br>JavaScript and Ruby are all written in C. Software like the early TCP/IP<br>networking implementations that made the Internet possible were written in C.<br>And the first web browsers were written in C. C made major advances in computer<br>architecture possible because operating systems, compilers, and utilities could<br>be re-compiled on a new architecture once they built a C compiler for the<br>architecture. So there is a very good chance that almost all the software that<br>you use was either written in C or written in a programming language that was<br>written in C. So we study C less as a programming language to use on a daily<br>basis and more as the foundation of modern software and computing. Thee<br>underlying book is copyright all rights reserved by AT&T but is being used in<br>this work under "fair use" because of the book's historical and scholarly<br>significance, its lack of availability, and lack of an accessible version of the<br>book. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/64vMqVLxRyvbKgGTF0Ru4V) |
| **CCO.us Podcast &#124; Medical Coding, Billing, Auditing & Risk Adjustment** | Welcome to the CCO.us Podcast, the podcast that peels back the layers of Medical<br>Coding, Billing, Auditing and Risk Adjustment. In this ongoing series, we'll<br>dive into the intricacies of medical coding with the guidance of one of the<br>industry's most esteemed experts, Alicia Scott, CPC, CPC-I, CRC. Alicia Scott is<br>a renowned figure in the world of medical coding and billing. With an impressive<br>background as a medical records abstractor, analyst, risk adjustment auditor,<br>and consultant, Alicia has established herself as a subject matter expert and<br>coach. Today, she holds the position of Director of Education at CCO.us, a<br>leading platform offering education in the Medical Coding, Billing, Auditing and<br>Risk Adjustment field, where aspiring professionals attain essential<br>certifications such as the CPC®, CRC, CPB® and more. Over the past five years,<br>Alicia has focused her expertise on provider education, bridging the gap between<br>healthcare providers and the often complex world of medical coding, specifically<br>navigating the challenges of the ICD-10 code set and Clinical Documentation<br>Improvement (CDI). Her mission has been to alleviate the frustration experienced<br>by providers and cultivate a strong, collaborative relationship between them and<br>the coders who translate their work into accurate coding. Throughout this<br>podcast series, Alicia will draw upon her extensive knowledge and teaching<br>experience at CCO.us, sharing invaluable insights on analyzing medical records<br>for accuracy and completeness, conducting precise coding activities, and<br>addressing common errors and issues that arise in the field. Discover how CCO.us<br>and Alicia Scott are revolutionizing the landscape of medical coding education.<br>Subscribe now to the CCO.us Podcast and embark on a journey towards mastering<br>medical coding with the guidance of a true industry luminary. | **Curious Kids Every Day: A Daily Educational Podcast** | Do you have a curious kid who asks you a million questions every day? Curious<br>Kids Every Day is an intentionally slow paced and interactive daily podcast for<br>kids AND their parents! My goal is to make learning fun for the whole family. My<br>name is Josh. I am a dad of 4 who loves to learn and teach. I am grateful for<br>all of the families who make Curious Kids Every Day a part of their daily<br>routine. I have noticed that most children's shows these days (even the<br>educational ones) are so loud, fast, and dumbed down. I guess they've always<br>been that way to a degree, but it is definitely worse now than when I was a kid.<br>This show is meant to reclaim a little bit of the sincerity and intentionality<br>of the best of children's programming. Specifically, I am inspired by Mister<br>Rogers' Neighborhood and Blue's Clues. I like how those shows are slow paced,<br>how they talk directly to the kids, and how they respect the listener's<br>intelligence. I intend for Curious Kids Every Day to do the same thing.<br>Basically, I am making the kind of show that I want my kids to enjoy. Give this<br>show a shot and watch how engaged your kid gets! I was amazed the first time<br>that I played it for my kids. Audio has a unique ability to capture the<br>imagination in a way that screens just cannot. Thank you for taking the time to<br>read this, and I hope that your family enjoys Curious Kids Every Day! For more<br>information about the show, along with a free printable with activity ideas that<br>don't involve screens: For activity sheets for every new episode of the show, go<br>to: | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/2ccnGAWAVdgt9WjzokDBpV) |

He has a background in software<br>engineering, works as an investor, and enjoys basketball and gaming.Jun, a<br>native Korean in his 30s, was born in Daegu, where he also spent his childhood<br>years. After moving to Seoul for college, he went to the US (Boston) for grad<br>school, but returned to Seoul after and has lived there ever since. | **Software Testing Unleashed - QA, DevEx & Quality Engineering** | Software testing is no longer just a phase—it’s the foundation of modern<br>engineering and your ultimate competitive advantage. Welcome to Software Testing<br>Unleashed, the weekly podcast for anyone dedicated to building better software,<br>faster. Hosted by Richard Seidl, renowned expert in software development and<br>testing, this show is your backstage pass to the tools, tactics, and trends<br>defining the next era of Quality Engineering. Whether you are a QA Engineer,<br>SDET, Developer, or Tech Leader, each week we bring you field-tested insights<br>from the brightest minds in the software universe to answer the industry’s<br>toughest questions: - Smart Automation: When should you automate, and when is it<br>a trap? - AI & ML in Testing: How do you maintain quality in a world of<br>non-deterministic code? - The "How Much" Dilemma: How much testing is actually<br>enough for your specific scale? - Architecture & DevEx: What makes a great<br>integration test and how do you improve developer experience? From scaling QA<br>strategies in enterprise projects to building your very first test suite, we<br>bridge the gap between complex theory and practical execution. We dive deep into<br>CI/CD, Cloud-native complexity, and the future of manual vs. automated testing.<br>🚀 Ready to unleash the next level of quality? Hit play, subscribe, and join a<br>global movement of software professionals shipping with confidence. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/1F4R2XkxbkI3v27rXbruyn) |
| **Software Testing with Fexingo: QA, Automation, and Reliable Software Engineering** | Lucas and Luna examine the discipline of software testing as a strategic<br>function, not a checkbox. Each episode focuses on a specific testing practice —<br>from unit test coverage metrics and integration test design patterns to the<br>economics of test automation ROI and the psychology of flaky test triage. They<br>walk through real-world case studies: how Stripe reduced regression defects by<br>40% with contract testing, the trade-offs between Selenium and Cypress for<br>e-commerce checkouts, and why Google's test size taxonomy (small/medium/large)<br>forces better architectural decisions. Lucas explains the math behind mutation<br>testing scores and the diminishing returns of 100% code coverage; Luna<br>challenges him on how to sell testing budgets to skeptical product managers and<br>when manual exploratory testing still outperforms automation. They also explore<br>testing philosophies — shift-left vs. shift-right, risk-based testing<br>prioritization, and the role of AI in generating test oracles. The listener<br>leaves each episode with a specific framework or heuristic: how to evaluate a<br>test suite's health, when to kill a test, or how to design a QA career path that<br>doesn't plateau. No generic advice — just the numbers, the names, and the<br>debates that shape reliable software. What does it actually take to build a<br>testing culture that prevents defects instead of just catching them?<br>#SoftwareTesting #QA #TestAutomation #UnitTests #IntegrationTesting<br>#RegressionTesting #Cypress #Selenium #MutationTesting #TestCoverage #ShiftLeft<br>#FlakyTests #TestROI #ContinuousTesting #SoftwareEngineering #Technology<br>#FexingoBusiness #BusinessPodcast Keep every episode free: | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/033kZENO5QBvazIFdvyyCw) |

| **Starting Up: How I Built & Sold a SaaS Company for Millions Without Coding Skills &#124; Jay Sensi** | I built and sold a software company for millions. I've never written a line of<br>code.Starting Up is the definitive playbook for non-technical founders who want<br>to build, scale, and exit software companies—without needing technical<br>skills.I'm Jay Sensi. In 2012, I had an idea for My College Roomie (later Campus<br>Kaizen)—a college roommate matching platform. The problem? I had zero coding<br>skills, no technical co-founder, a full-time job I couldn't quit, and limited<br>capital.Everyone said I needed to be technical to build a software company.<br>Everyone was wrong.Over the next 10 years, I validated the market, learned to<br>spec software without technical knowledge, built strategic partnerships that<br>created 10x growth, scaled to multi-million dollar ARR while working full-time,<br>sold to a private equity firm, and retired at 40.Now I'm documenting the entire<br>journey—the strategies that worked, the mistakes that cost six figures, and the<br>exact roadmap from idea to exit.WHAT YOU'LL LEARN:- How to build software as a<br>non-technical founder- Validating SaaS ideas before heavy investment- Hiring and<br>managing developers when you can't code- Writing product specs without technical<br>knowledge- Partnership strategies that drive exponential growth- Scaling a<br>business nights and weekends while working full-time- How to know when to sell<br>(and how to actually do it)- What private equity acquisition and due diligence<br>really look like- Real mistakes, real numbers, real lessons from building Campus<br>KaizenWHO THIS IS FOR:Aspiring SaaS founders who aren't technical and think they<br>can't do this. Entrepreneurs with software ideas who don't know where to start.<br>Business owners looking to add software to their offerings. Anyone building a<br>startup while working a day job.You don't need to code. | **The Programming Languages Podcast with Fexingo: Python, Rust, JavaScript, and Modern Coding** | Every line of code is a decision, and every programming language encodes a<br>philosophy. In The Programming Languages Podcast, Lucas and Luna move past<br>syntax flame wars to examine the actual trade-offs behind Python, Rust,<br>JavaScript, and the modern coding stack. Each episode dissects a specific<br>language feature, framework choice, or ecosystem shift — from Rust's borrow<br>checker and memory safety guarantees to JavaScript's type system evolution with<br>TypeScript, and Python's dominance in machine learning versus its performance<br>bottlenecks. They ground every discussion in real-world benchmarks, open-source<br>projects like Deno and PyPy, and case studies from companies that bet on one<br>language over another. Lucas brings the reporter's instinct for clarity and hard<br>numbers; Luna tests those findings with the engineer's skepticism and hands-on<br>experience. You will walk away understanding not just what a language does, but<br>why it was designed that way, and when you should — or shouldn't — use it. What<br>does Rust's ownership model teach us about concurrency? Is JavaScript's<br>flexibility a feature or a bug for large-scale systems? Can Python ever overcome<br>the GIL? This is the podcast for developers who are tired of cargo-culting and<br>want to think critically about the tools they use every day. #PythonProgramming<br>#RustLang #JavaScript #TypeScript #ModernCoding #MemorySafety<br>#ProgrammingLanguages #SoftwareEngineering #TechDebate #OpenSource<br>#CodeBenchmarks #Deno #PyPy #GIL #BorrowChecker #Business #FexingoBusiness<br>#Technology Keep every episode free: | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/033kZC6PjHJ8RZkr2saJL6) |

| **The Software Engineering Podcast with Fexingo: Code, Architecture, and Engineering Best Practices** | Lucas and Luna sit down at side-by-side laptops to talk about the craft of<br>building software. Each episode picks a single engineering challenge —<br>optimizing a database query for latency, designing a fault-tolerant microservice<br>boundary, refactoring a legacy monolith without breaking production — and walks<br>through the trade-offs with real code examples and benchmark numbers. They<br>debate testing strategies (integration vs. end-to-end, when to mock), revisit<br>classic papers on distributed systems and data structures, and trace how<br>architectural decisions cascade into operational costs. The show serves senior<br>developers, staff engineers, and technical leads who want to hear reasoned,<br>specific conversations about trade-offs and rigor — not hype about the latest<br>framework. Lucas brings the journalist's habit of asking why a team chose one<br>pattern over another; Luna pushes back with real-world failure stories from her<br>own career. Together, they treat software engineering as a discipline of<br>explicit decisions and measurable outcomes. No hot takes, no 'best practices'<br>without context. Just two engineers thinking out loud about how to build systems<br>that last. What does it actually cost to ship a feature with 99.99% uptime — and<br>when is that the wrong target? #SoftwareEngineering #SystemArchitecture<br>#CodeQuality #DistributedSystems #DatabaseOptimization #Refactoring<br>#TestingStrategy #Microservices #Monolith #PerformanceEngineering #CleanCode<br>#TechDebt #EngineeringCulture #PairProgramming #FexingoBusiness #BusinessPodcast<br>#Technology #EngineeringBestPractices Keep every episode free: | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/033kZhlmKVaA53Jy5Wo45u) |

Scott, Wes, and CJ dig into whether Void is the Rails moment<br>JavaScript has been waiting for, or just shiny Cloudflare lock-in with a bow on<br>it. | [↗](https://syntax.fm/show/991/vite-s-bet-on-cloudflare-void-framework) |

| **دوره آموزشی مبانی معماری نرم افزار &#124; Software Architecture Fundamentals** | امروزه، سیستم‌های نرم‌افزاری به عنوان یکی از پیچیده‌ترین ساختارهایی شناخته<br>می‌شوند که تاکنون توسط انسان ساخته شده‌اند. سیستم‌هایی که در کنار تمام<br>پیچیدگی‌های فنی، باید از نظر تجاری هم موفق باشند و برای کسب‌و‌کار ارزش خلق کنند.<br>در عین حال، تیم‌ها و سازمان‌های توسعه‌دهنده نرم-افزار، باید موضوعات فرهنگی و<br>تیمی را هم در طراحی این سیستم‌ها در نظر بگیرند. درهم‌تنیدگی و پیچیدگی طراحی چنین<br>سیستم‌هایی، می‌تواند منجر به تولید نرم‌افزاری نارکارآمد (از لحاظ رفتاری و کیفی)<br>و در نتیجه افزایش شدید هزینه‌های توسعه و نگهداری و یا حتی شکست پروژه شود.معماری<br>نرم‌افزار را می‌توان یکی از کلیدی‌ترین حوزه‌های طراحی نرم‌افزار دانست. معماری<br>نرم‌افزار، نه یک حوزه صرفا فنی، بلکه یک حوزه کاملا راهبردی و استراتژیک است.<br>تصمیمات حوزه معماری نرم‌افزار، تصمیمات کلیدی و فنی-استراتژیک هستند که می‌توانند<br>در موفقیت و یا عدم موفقیت پروژه نقش کلیدی ایفا کنند.در طول این دوره، موضوعات<br>مختلفی از جمله تعریف معماری نرم‌افزار، نقش و اهمیت آن، ابعاد مختلف تاثیر معماری<br>نرم‌افزار، و حوزه Architectural Design را بررسی خواهیم کرد. همچنین به بررسی<br>معماری نرم‌افزار، از بعد کیفی میپردازیم و مولفه‌ها و مشخصه‌های کیفی را به تفکیک<br>بررسی میکنیم. سپس به سراغ چرخه تولید نرم‌افزار می‌رویم و نقش و تاثیر معماری و<br>همینطور وظایف و کارکرد معمار را بررسی میکنیم.این دوره، بر مفاهیم اساسی و بنیادین<br>تمرکز دارد. | **Let's Talk AppSecOps** | Agile DevOps, Cloud Deployment, Microservices, and Open Source have all<br>dramatically accelerated application delivery and complexity. Today’s AppSec<br>teams, outnumbered by as much as 100:1 by developers, depend on a collection of<br>point security products and siloed manual processes. This leaves them struggling<br>to gain the visibility, insight, and process scale they need to identify and<br>protect the always changing and growing application risk surface. This resulting<br>AppSec Chaos means applications ship fast without the assurance of shipping<br>securely, leaving the organization at risk of breaches and losses.Welcome to the<br>Let's Talk AppSecOps show, where we bring discussions with security thought<br>leaders and practitioners and software development minds from leading<br>enterprises to talk about their concerns, case studies, and best practices in<br>operationalizing application security. This show is hosted by ArmorCode experts.<br>It talks about how security and development teams can work together and<br>implement practical security measures. Mark Lambert is a AppSecOps Evangelist<br>and VP of Products at ArmorCode, breaking down the communication barrier between<br>Developers and Security experts to help organizations adopt and scale security<br>practices within their development teams.Mark is passionate about applying<br>technology innovations to solving real world business problems. Luis's diverse skill set includes the ability to<br>explain technology to non-technical audiences, set up a “pizza box” in a<br>freezer, cook a perfect elk steak over a mesquite-fueled grill, and spend late<br>nights in a war-room! Avid Secure built a marketing-led AI-powered multi-cloud security and<br>compliance platform. Nikhil holds an MBA from Columbia Business School and BS<br>and MS in Computer Science. LingRaj Patil is the Head of Marketing at ArmorCode<br>and a renowned speaker and business growth leader with 20+ years of experience<br>spanning engineering and marketing in Seed, Series A/B/C/D, Unicorn, and Fortune<br>500 companies. | **Notions of Progress** | The idea of progress — that humanity advances through time toward something<br>better — has been a contested assumption throughout history. The question of<br>whether we as a species, a nation, or as individuals are progressing is being<br>interrogated with a renewed urgency that the present moment demands. Whether<br>that advancement is real, illusory, unevenly distributed, or simply beside the<br>point depends on who is asking, from where, and by what measure. Notions of<br>Progress is a podcast that takes those questions seriously, tracing how the idea<br>of progress has been understood, contested, and reimagined from antiquity to the<br>age of artificial intelligence. The series moves from the ancient Greeks’<br>ambivalence about technological change, through the Enlightenment’s confidence<br>in cumulative human reason, to the contemporary moment in which artificial<br>intelligence has made the question of progress newly urgent. When machines<br>appear to learn, create, and reason, the assumptions buried inside the word<br>“progress” — about agency, direction, and human advancement — are no longer<br>abstract. Tracing how those assumptions formed, and how they have been<br>challenged across centuries, is the work of this podcast. Rather than<br>prescribing a position, it surfaces the debates — examining how thinkers from<br>Hesiod to Hayek, from Plato to Peter Haff, have understood what it means for<br>humanity to move forward, at what cost, for whom, and by whose definition. The<br>thread connecting Plato’s Academy to the age of artificial intelligence is not a<br>straight line of accumulation — it is a recurring argument about whether<br>progress is something driven by human agency, providence, or an artificial<br>consciousness we project onto history rather than find in it. Host Marshall<br>Madow brings an unusual dual formation to these questions. His MA in History<br>from Cambridge University — where his thesis examined Georges Sorel’s<br>epistemology of myth and the role of ideas in animating collective action — gave<br>him a grounding in how belief systems about progress can function in place of<br>rational thinking as historical forces. His MSc from Oxford University’s Saïd<br>Business School, specializing in Complexity Science and Leadership, introduced<br>him to the question from the other direction: how systems evolve, how change<br>propagates through institutions, and why linear models of advancement are often<br>insufficient in explaining complex phenomena. The two areas of study point<br>toward the same problem from opposite ends. Notions of Progress is the inquiry<br>that connects them. Marshall approaches the podcast as a scholar, researcher,<br>and curator — seeking to surface a pluralistic and wide-ranging spectrum of<br>ideas rather than prescribing or advocating one position over another. Contact:<br>marshall@ Social: @notionsofprogress on Instagram · @NotionsProgress on<br>X/Twitter Web: | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/5WgTlVMBfFzrIQwqkqhiD9) |
| **OGTalks** | The OptimusGavin (OG) ecosystem is built upon the foundational philosophy of<br>"Infrastructure Supremacy," a strategic approach that deliberately trades easy<br>application-level coding for military-grade infrastructure performance. Unlike<br>traditional enterprise systems that prioritize "application comfort" by relying<br>on bloated monoliths or microservices written in single, developer-friendly<br>languages like Java or Node.js, OG utilizes a highly specialized stack of<br>high-throughput and memory-safe languages such as Rust and Go. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/033DgEIHRqMoXTxJzDefi3) |

| **AWS Certified Security Specialist Podcast** | Welcome to the 'AWS Certified Security Specialist Podcast' where we considered<br>every domain, task statement, knowledge and skill to build a complete audio<br>study guide for the exam 'AWS Certified Security - Specialty (SCS-C02) Exam'.<br>Please like (thumbs up) or provide positive feedback as that would be helpful.<br>Let me know what domain or task statements you would like more content in and<br>will endeavor to get new episodes available for free and subscribers soon.<br>Domain 1 is totally free and the remaining domains initial tasks are also super<br>free episodes. **** Subscribe on Apple Podcasts to access the full course !!!<br>**** Domain 1: Threat Detection and Incident Response focuses on designing<br>comprehensive incident response plans that incorporate AWS best practices,<br>cloud-specific incident handling, and clearly defined roles and responsibilities<br>using the AWS Security Finding Format (ASFF). This domain emphasizes<br>implementing credential invalidation and rotation strategies through services<br>like IAM and AWS Secrets Manager, while ensuring proper resource isolation<br>during security events. Critical skills include deploying and integrating<br>security services such as Security Hub, GuardDuty, Macie, Inspector, Config,<br>Detective, and IAM Access Analyzer with native AWS services and third-party<br>tools through EventBridge. The domain covers detecting security threats and<br>anomalies using AWS managed security services, employing correlation techniques<br>to join data across services, and creating visualizations to identify unusual<br>patterns while centralizing security findings for comprehensive analysis. Domain<br>2: Security Logging and Monitoring centers on designing and implementing robust<br>monitoring and alerting systems to address security events using services like<br>CloudWatch and EventBridge for automated responses. This includes analyzing<br>architectures to identify monitoring requirements, setting up automated auditing<br>tools, and defining appropriate metrics and thresholds for alert generation. The<br>domain encompasses comprehensive logging solutions utilizing VPC Flow Logs, DNS<br>logs, CloudTrail, and CloudWatch Logs with proper lifecycle management and<br>retention policies. Key competencies include troubleshooting logging<br>configurations, identifying missing logs, managing access permissions for<br>logging services, and designing log analysis solutions using tools like Athena,<br>CloudWatch Logs Insights, and Security Hub insights to identify patterns<br>indicating anomalies and known threats. Domain 3: Infrastructure Security<br>emphasizes implementing security controls across edge services, networks, and<br>compute workloads to protect against common attacks and exploits. Edge security<br>involves leveraging AWS WAF, load balancers, Route 53, CloudFront, and Shield to<br>create layered defense strategies against threats like OWASP Top 10 and DDoS<br>attacks, while applying geographic and rate-limiting restrictions. Network<br>security focuses on VPC security mechanisms including security groups, network<br>ACLs, and Network Firewall, along with inter-VPC connectivity through Transit<br>Gateway and VPC endpoints to keep data off the public internet. Compute workload<br>security involves provisioning and maintaining EC2 instances with proper<br>patching, vulnerability scanning through Inspector and ECR, implementing IAM<br>instance roles, creating hardened AMIs, and applying host-based security<br>mechanisms while securely managing secrets and credentials. Domain 4: Identity<br>and Access Ma | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/6sIkngwQ9DCOQkiJze3v4y) |

| **Cargo Cult** | Cargo Cult is a podcast hosted by Naomi Karavani and Michele Greenstein that<br>pulls back the curtain on these often forgotten forces shaping our digital<br>world.A cargo cult is what happens when people copy the visible parts of<br>something they think is successful — without understanding the deeper systems<br>that actually make it work.The term comes from World War II. Allied forces<br>landed on remote Pacific islands, bringing with them cargo: food, medicine,<br>equipment, and more. To the islanders, this sudden arrival of wealth and<br>technology seemed magical. They noticed planes landing on runways, soldiers in<br>uniforms, and people waving flags. After the war ended and the soldiers left,<br>some islanders built fake airstrips and wooden control towers, hoping the cargo<br>would return. They copied the form, not the function.Today, “cargo cult” is used<br>as a metaphor — especially in science, business, and tech — to describe similar<br>behavior: loving the surface level symbols of surface of success but missing<br>what actually made it happen.So what does this have to do with Big Tech?A lot.In<br>the tech world, there’s a tendency to imitate what successful companies look<br>like on the outside — without really understanding what made them powerful in<br>the first place. Teams copy Google’s culture, Amazon’s strategies, or Facebook’s<br>experiments — but often miss the context that made those things effective.We<br>believe the real engines of Big Tech’s rise are rarely talked about: deep<br>partnerships with the state, access to elite networks, and the invisible support<br>of powerful institutions. Our podcast, Cargo Cult, is about exposing this<br>reality — and pushing back on the myth that tech’s biggest winners got there<br>through merit, hustle, or pure technical innovation alone.The truth is, many of<br>these companies were shaped from the beginning by their relationships with the<br>U.S. government and intelligence community. Google got early funding connected<br>to DARPA. Amazon’s cloud arm, AWS, landed massive contracts with the CIA and<br>Department of Defense. Palantir was funded by In-Q-Tel, the CIA’s venture fund.<br>Microsoft has long been a major federal contractor and now runs one of the<br>largest government cloud platforms on the planet.These companies weren’t just<br>building better software. They were aligning with the long-term agendas of state<br>power — especially around surveillance, data collection, and behavior control.<br>And yet, all over the world, tech founders and governments keep chasing and<br>advertising the aesthetic of Big Tech: innovation labs, AI initiatives, moonshot<br>pitches, and “disruption” — without understanding the political and financial<br>structures that actually enabled these companies to dominate.This is the heart<br>of cargo cult thinking.It’s not that Big Tech didn’t have talented engineers or<br>good products. And we have nothing against open-plan offices or slick pitch<br>decks. But the story we’re often told — of scrappy underdogs changing the world<br>with nothing but brains and code — leaves out everything that made that kind of<br>success possible in the first place. Regulatory capture, government funding,<br>military contracts, surveillance programs, the workings of capitalism, and<br>aligning with long-term elite agendas — these are the real foundations of Big<br>Tech, and it's the stuff we like to talk about on the show. We also talk about<br>pop culture sometimes.____Complain at cargocultpod@ | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/4kE3vG60wvKyoRf2RMfkfM) |

| **Cloud Computing with Fexingo: AWS, Azure, GCP, and Modern Infrastructure Conversations** | Cloud computing is the backbone of modern business, but the landscape is<br>shifting fast. Lucas and Luna cut through the vendor noise to examine the<br>real-world strategies behind AWS, Azure, and GCP — from multi-cloud<br>architectures to edge computing and container orchestration. Each episode takes<br>a single infrastructure decision, like choosing a database service or designing<br>for disaster recovery, and traces its implications for cost, latency, and<br>developer productivity. Lucas brings deep technical fluency and a journalist's<br>skepticism toward marketing claims; Luna tests each argument against case<br>studies from companies like Netflix, Capital One, and Adobe. They don't just<br>compare prices — they explore trade-offs in lock-in, compliance, and operational<br>complexity. Whether dissecting a Kubernetes outage or the economics of<br>serverless, the conversation is always grounded in concrete specs and real<br>bills. This is the podcast for engineering leaders and cloud architects who want<br>to make informed bets, not follow trends. But what happens when the cloud giants<br>change their pricing mid-contract? That's exactly the kind of tension Lucas and<br>Luna live inside — and the conversation that will reshape how you think about<br>infrastructure. #CloudComputing #AWS #Azure #GCP #MultiCloud #Kubernetes<br>#Serverless #EdgeComputing #DevOps #Infrastructure #CloudArchitecture<br>#DigitalTransformation #Technology #CloudStrategy #FexingoBusiness<br>#BusinessPodcast #TechPodcast #CloudInfrastructure Keep every episode free: | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/033kZgscF7qw4wP3h4Tt8L) |

| **Google** | Google LLC (/ˈɡuːɡəl/ ⓘ GOO-ghəl) is an American multinational<br>corporation and technology company focusing on online advertising, search<br>engine technology, cloud computing, computer software, quantum<br>computing, e-commerce, consumer electronics, and artificial<br>intelligence (AI).[9] It has been referred to as "the most powerful company in<br>the world"[10] and is one of the world's most valuable brands due to its market<br>dominance, data collection, and technological advantages in the field of<br>AI.[11][12][13] Google's parent company, Alphabet Inc. is one of the five Big<br>Tech companies, alongside Amazon, Apple, Meta, and Microsoft.Google was founded<br>on September 4, 1998, by American computer scientists Larry Page and Sergey<br>Bring while they were PhD students at Stanford University in California.<br>Together, they own about 14% of its publicly listed shares and control 56% of<br>its stockholder voting power through super-voting stock. The company<br>went public via an initial public offering (IPO) in 2004. In 2015, Google was<br>reorganized as a wholly owned subsidiary of Alphabet Inc. Google is Alphabet's<br>largest subsidiary and is a holding company for Alphabet's internet properties<br>and interests. Sundar Pichai was appointed CEO of Google on October 24, 2015,<br>replacing Larry Page, who became the CEO of Alphabet. On December 3, 2019,<br>Pichai also became the CEO of Alphabet.[14]The company has since rapidly grown<br>to offer a multitude of products and services beyond Google Search, many of<br>which hold dominant market positions. These products address a wide range of use<br>cases, including email (Gmail), navigation (Waze & Maps), cloud<br>computing (Cloud), web navigation (Chrome), video sharing (YouTube),<br>productivity (Workspace), operating systems (Android), cloud<br>storage (Drive), language translation (Translate), photo storage<br>(Photos), videotelephony (Meet), smart<br>home (Nest), smartphones (Pixel), wearable technology (Pixel<br>Watch & Fitbit), music streaming (YouTube Music), video on demand (YouTube<br>TV), AI (Google Assistant & Gemini), machine learning APIs (TensorFlow), AI<br>chips (TPU), and more. Discontinued Google<br>products include gaming (Stadia), Glass, Google+, Reader, Play<br>Music, Nexus, Hangouts, and Inbox by Gmail.[15][16]Google's other ventures<br>outside of internet services and consumer electronics include quantum<br>computing (Sycamore), self-driving cars (Waymo, formerly the Google Self-Driving<br>Car Project), smart cities (Sidewalk Labs), and transformer models (Google<br>DeepMind).[17]Google and YouTube are the two most-visited websites worldwide<br>followed by Facebook and X (formerly known as Twitter). Google is also the<br>largest search engine, mapping and navigation application, email<br>provider, office suite, online video platform, photo and cloud<br>storage provider, mobile operating system, web browser, machine learning<br>framework, and AI virtual assistant provider in the world as measured by market<br>share.[18] On the list of most valuable brands, Google is ranked second<br>by Forbes[19] and fourth by Interbrand.[20] It has received significant<br>criticism involving issues such as privacy concerns, tax<br>avoidance, censorship, search neutrality, antitrust and abuse of<br>its monopoly position. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/3cyBbC0U8Q2wFuYe3oFEQn) |

Across six episodes, I walk through what actually changes when you stop<br>prompting and start engineering a workflow: what Copilot really reads when you<br>hit Enter, why more context isn't better context, how to break one endless chat<br>into subagents that coordinate, how to teach Copilot the things only your team<br>knows, and how MCP turns it from a chat box into a workflow engine wired to your<br>whole stack. No hype, no "AI will replace you" theatre — just the mental models<br>and patterns I use day to day on a real React and Azure DevOps codebase. If you<br>want Copilot to behave less like a toy and more like a teammate, start here. As<br>proud supporters of a non-profit movement created by podcasters, for podcasters,<br>about podcasters, we provide all this—the podcast, live mentorship, and<br>coaching—FREE to Podcasters! Join LIVE every Wednesday Noon PT.This year, in our<br>live sessions, we've tackled every critical facet of the content creation<br>ecosystem, starting with the absolute bedrock: Mindset and Foundation. Your<br>hosts and mentors, Tracy Hazzard and Tom Hazzard; founders of the podcast<br>hosting and podcast AI production platform, have each recorded over 1500 podcast<br>episodes, launched over 1200 shows guiding pros like you (and helped new<br>creators avoid common pitfalls) through establishing a robust monetization<br>strategy, building a scalable business model, and laser-focusing your unique<br>brand story. We hammer home the non-negotiable importance of a clear mission,<br>vision, and values to ensure your content generates authentic revenue.To keep<br>your show growing, we cut through the noise with Content Strategy and Planning.<br>No more haphazard ideas—we equip you with a systematic approach. Our coaching<br>covers everything from pinpointing your target audience and dissecting their<br>pain points to building a robust content calendar that actually works. We break<br>down how to ideate compelling topics, structure engaging episodes that hook<br>listeners, and craft captivating show notes that drive real engagement. Plus, we<br>introduce frameworks for evergreen content, ensuring your efforts keep paying<br>dividends long after initial publication.Production and Promotion are central<br>themes because, let's be real, you need to sound and be seen like a pro. We<br>demystify the technical aspects, exploring essential equipment, advanced<br>recording techniques, and editing best practices for professional-grade audio.<br>Beyond the mic, we dive deep into the art of promotion: effective launch<br>strategies, leveraging social media like a boss, cross-promotion tactics, and<br>SEO optimization for maximum discoverability. We show you how to repurpose<br>content across platforms, extending your reach and impact without reinventing<br>the wheel.Understanding your audience's journey is paramount, leading us to<br>Audience Engagement and Community Building. We equip you with strategies to<br>cultivate a loyal listenership, drive those crucial reviews, and build a vibrant<br>community around your brand. We unlock the power of interactive content, Q&A<br>sessions, and leveraging listener feedback to refine your offerings and forge<br>stronger connections.Finally, we bring it all together with Monetization and<br>Business Growth. We unveil diverse revenue streams beyond traditional<br>sponsorships, including leveraging your podcast to sell products, services, and<br>courses. We explore affiliate marketing, membership models, and the undeniable<br>power of lead generation through your content. We provide the insights you need<br>to scale your business, build a high-performing team, and optimize your<br>processes for sustainable, explosive growth.Our style? Energetic, direct, and<br>relentlessly practical. We bring you real-world examples, personal anecdotes,<br>and step-by-step guidance—all designed to ignite your action and deliver<br>tangible results. Feed Your Brand isn't just about launching a podcast; it's<br>about building a powerful media asset that fuels your business, amplifies your<br>message, and solidifies your position as an authority in your field. Join us and<br>unlock the immense potential of strategic content. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/6GHSptVZWE7Vw6KvVx7bhu) |
| **Financial Behavior Thought Leaders** | What makes a keynote speaker truly unforgettable? What happens behind the scenes<br>before they step onto the stage? And, as a financial professional or event<br>planner, how do you know which speaker is the right fit for your audience?<br>Welcome to Financial Behavior Thought Leaders–the podcast that gives you an<br>exclusive, behind-the-scenes look at the most sought-after speakers in financial<br>services. This show is hosted by Dr. Mary Bell Carlson, founder of Financial<br>Behavior Keynote Group, where we represent the best financial thought leaders<br>and speakers in the industry. Every day, financial advisors, event organizers,<br>and industry professionals are searching for dynamic speakers to educate,<br>inspire, and engage their audiences—but how do you find the perfect one? That’s<br>where this podcast comes in. Every other week, I sit down with top financial<br>speakers to uncover their journey, explore their most powerful keynotes, and<br>give you a front-row seat to the stories that make their talks unforgettable.<br>You’ll hear insider insights, a preview of their message, and even a<br>behind-the-scenes look at what it’s really like to work with them. Listen every<br>other week for conversations around: Financial Behavior & Psychology Money<br>decisions aren’t just logical—they’re deeply emotional and psychological. We<br>explore the behavioral biases, habits, and mindsets that shape financial<br>choices, helping financial professionals better understand both their clients<br>and themselves. From the latest research in behavioral finance to practical<br>applications in advising, we uncover how psychology influences money and how to<br>reshape financial behaviors for better outcomes. Financial Wellness True<br>financial success goes beyond wealth—it’s about overall well-being. This podcast<br>dives into holistic financial wellness, addressing not just numbers but stress,<br>confidence, and long-term security. We discuss strategies to help clients<br>achieve balance in their financial lives while ensuring advisors have the tools<br>to promote sustainable financial health. Emotional Intelligence & Resiliency<br>Financial professionals face unique pressures, from emotional client<br>interactions to regulatory demands. We focus on resilience, stress management,<br>and mental well-being, offering insights on how to thrive in a high-pressure<br>industry while maintaining personal fulfillment and balance. Communication We<br>dive into communication strategies that build trust, foster engagement, and help<br>financial professionals navigate difficult money conversations. Whether it's<br>coaching clients through emotional decisions or refining your storytelling<br>skills, we explore ways to make financial discussions more impactful. Practice<br>Management Running a successful financial practice requires more than technical<br>expertise. We discuss best practices for growing and managing a financial<br>advisory business, from operational efficiency to client experience. Whether<br>you're looking to improve workflows, scale your firm, or enhance client<br>relationships, we provide insights to help you build a thriving, sustainable<br>practice. Marketing & Technology for Financial Advisors From digital branding<br>and content marketing to fintech innovations and AI-driven client insights, we<br>provide practical ways to modernize and grow your advisory practice. Whether<br>you’re planning a client event, booking a major conference keynote, or just want<br>to learn from some of the brightest minds in financial services, Financial<br>Behavior Thought Leaders is your all-access pass to the best in the business.<br>Subscribe now and join me as we go beyond the podium to meet the voices shaping<br>the future of financial thought leadership. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/22V6u4N57QOZ0rbHEPgn2G) |

| **JakSeDostatDoMedii.cz Podcast: GEO Optimalizace, AI Marketing & SEO 2026 - Buďte Viditelní První!** | JakSeDostatDoMedii.cz Podcast: GEO Optimalizace, AI Marketing & SEO 2026 - Buďte<br>Viditelní První! je první a největší český podcast zaměřený na Generative Engine<br>Optimization (GEO), AI vyhledávače a budoucnost online marketingu. Pokud vás<br>Google přestává bavit a chcete dominovat v ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI<br>Overviews a dalších AI platformách, jste na správném místě.Proč právě teď<br>potřebujete GEO?Představte si, že vaši zákazníci už negoogleí. Místo toho se<br>ptají ChatGPT: "Jakou SEO agenturu v Praze doporučuješ?" A vaše firma tam není.<br>Vůbec. Zatímco vaše konkurence je citována jako první volba.To není sci-fi. To<br>je realita 2026. Podle aktuálních dat používá 48% českých firem generativní AI<br>systematicky - více než průměr EU. Gartner předpovídá 25% pokles tradičního<br>Google vyhledávání do konce roku. AI search není budoucnost. Je přítomnost. A<br>většina českých firem to ještě neví.V tomto podcastu vám ukážeme, jak být mezi<br>prvními, kdo ovládnou Generative Engine Optimization a AI marketing dřív, než to<br>udělá vaše se naučíte v každé epizodě?GEO Strategie a Taktiky: Detailní návody,<br>jak optimalizovat obsah pro AI vyhledávače. Od strukturovaných dat ( JSON-LD)<br>přes faktickou hustotu až po earned media building. Vše prakticky, testovatelně,<br>s českými příklady.AI SEO 2026: Jak se liší optimalizace pro ChatGPT, Perplexity<br>AI a Google AI Overviews. Která platforms preferuje jaké zdroje. Jak měřit AI<br>viditelnost bez Google Analytics. Jaké metriky sledovat (citační frekvence,<br>source diversity, share of voice).Online Marketing v Éře AI: Content marketing<br>strategie pro AI parsování. Jak psát články, které AI citují. E-E-A-T framework<br>pro umělou inteligenci. Topic clusters a sémantická stopa. Keyword research pro<br>generativní enginy.Technical GEO: Implementace schema markup, validace přes Rich<br>Results Test, optimalizace site speed pro AI crawlery, strukturování obsahu pro<br>syntézu, FAQ strategie pro featured snippets v AI odpovědích.Case Studies a<br>Data: Reálné české firmy, jejich GEO implementace a výsledky. Kolik stojí GEO<br>kampaň. Jak dlouho trvá vidět první citace. ROI z investice do AI visibility.<br>Competitive analysis - kdo vyhrává v českém AI search prostoru.Trendy a<br>Budoucnost: Agentic AI, multimodální GEO (optimalizace obrázků a videí pro AI),<br>vertikálně specializované strategie (e-commerce GEO vs B2B SaaS GEO), emerging<br>platforms, regulace AI search v EU.Pro koho je tento podcast?SEO specialisty a<br>konzultanty, kteří vidí, že tradiční Google SEO už nestačí a potřebují rozšířit<br>skillset do GEOMajitele e-shopů a firem, jejichž zákazníci začínají hledat<br>doporučení v AI nástrojích místo v GoogleDigital marketery a content kreátory,<br>kteří chtějí produkovat obsah optimalizovaný pro AI citovatelnostAgentury, které<br>chtějí přidat GEO do svého portfolio služeb před konkurencíPodnikatele a<br>freelancery, kteří budují osobní brand a potřebují být viditelní tam, kde jejich<br>klienti skutečně hledajíProč poslouchat zrovna nás?JakSeDostatDoMedii.cz je<br>největší český zdroj o digitálním marketingu, SEO a AI nástrojích s desetitisíci<br>čtenářů měsíčně. | **So That's Why** | You've been told to drink eight glasses of water a day. You've chased 10,000<br>steps like it's some kind of biological law. You've checked your cholesterol<br>without being entirely sure what you're actually checking for. Most health<br>content tells you what to do. Nobody explains why. That's the gap So That's Why<br>was made to fill. Each week, Jen, Chris, and Matt take one everyday health<br>question — the kind that's been nagging at the back of your mind, or that you've<br>just accepted without thinking — and unpack the actual science behind it. Where<br>did this idea come from? What's really happening inside your body? And does the<br>evidence actually hold up? What they find is often surprising. The 10,000-steps<br>rule was invented by a Japanese marketing team in 1964. The<br>eight-glasses-of-water recommendation came from a misread document. The reason<br>some people turn tomato-red when they exercise has nothing to do with fitness —<br>it's about blood vessel density. The thing that makes you cry when you chop<br>onions was only properly understood in 2002. Cholesterol is in every single cell<br>of your body — so why the terrible reputation? The science is real, the research<br>is specific, and the conversations are genuinely fascinating. And the three<br>people having them have the backgrounds to get it right. Jen holds a PhD in<br>biochemistry and molecular biology. She asks the questions you're thinking —<br>informed ones, not naive ones — and keeps the conversation grounded in the human<br>experience of all this biology. Chris is a formulation scientist with over 30<br>years of experience. He's read the studies, knows the mechanisms, and has the<br>analogies that make complex biology actually click. Matt looks at the science<br>and asks what it means for real people, with real lives, real schedules, and no<br>time for perfectionism. Together they hit that sweet spot between too technical<br>to understand and so simplified it's not actually true anymore. Getting there,<br>it turns out, is harder than it sounds. So That's Why doesn't give you a list of<br>rules to follow. It doesn't shame you for the things you haven't been doing. It<br>explains the mechanism — the actual biology — so you can make decisions that fit<br>your life, rather than just following advice that might not apply to you at all.<br>Episodes run about 20 minutes. They're built for commutes, workouts, or cooking<br>dinner. By the end of each one, you'll be able to explain the answer to someone<br>else — which is the whole point. New episodes every week. Subscribe and find out<br>why. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/0LvnastPKwbj9b1JmcZ8Bt) |

| **eCommerce Fastlane: Shopify Growth Strategies—Where AI Efficiency Meets Human Connection** | Running a Shopify store? You already know success isn't about finding the right<br>app—it's about knowing which strategies actually work at your specific stage of<br>growth. I'm Steve Hutt, and I've lived every level of the ecommerce journey.<br>From eBay Power Seller shipping products from my garage, to co-founding and<br>scaling (an optical DTC startup) through to a successful exit, then spending 6+<br>years as a Shopify Merchant Success Manager helping 100+ brands from startup to<br>enterprise scale, including working with teams at Tentree, Dr. Squatch,<br>Bulletproof Coffee, Salt & Straw Ice Cream, MUD/WTR, Bokksu, and many more.<br>Here's what makes this podcast different: I've been on both sides of the table.<br>6+ years working inside Shopify gave me access to what's coming before most<br>merchants know it exists. That combination of insider access plus real<br>operator experience gives you a perspective you won't find anywhere else. Since<br>2018, eCommerce Fastlane has delivered over 430 episodes and more than 2 million<br>downloads to ambitious Shopify founders and marketers. Every week, I chat with<br>operators, developers, and experts who share what's actually moving the needle<br>right now—not theory, but patterns I've seen work consistently across dozens of<br>brands at multiple stages. Whether you're launching your first store or scaling<br>past seven figures, every episode gives you stage-aware strategies you can<br>implement this week. We dig into Shopify apps, digital marketing, retention<br>tactics, AI tools, and the operational stuff that actually reduces<br>overwhelm—while building customer loyalty that lasts. You'll learn how to<br>leverage emerging tech without losing what makes your brand unique. You'll also<br>hear real founder stories, including lessons from my own journey building and<br>exiting a DTC brand, plus insights from guests across 430+ episodes who've been<br>exactly where you are. This isn't content for content's sake. It's what you'd<br>get sitting at a mastermind table with someone who's built, scaled, and<br>exited—and helped hundreds of others do the same. | **DevOps Daily with Fexingo: CI/CD, Kubernetes, and Modern Software Operations** | Lucas and Luna dissect the daily realities of DevOps, from CI/CD pipeline design<br>to Kubernetes cluster management and the human systems that keep software<br>running. Each episode grounds abstract principles in real incidents—a failed<br>deployment at a major retailer, a postmortem from a cloud outage, a<br>configuration drift disaster—and traces the operational decisions that turned<br>them around. Lucas brings the technical precision of a working engineer, while<br>Luna pushes on the team dynamics, cost trade-offs, and organizational<br>bottlenecks that separate resilient operations from fragile ones. They discuss<br>monitoring strategies, incident response playbooks, infrastructure-as-code<br>trade-offs, and the cultural friction between development velocity and<br>operational stability—always with concrete examples, never with buzzwords. This<br>is the show for engineers, SREs, and platform leads who want to hear two<br>seasoned practitioners argue through the hard choices: when to rewrite vs.<br>patch, how much observability is enough, and how to keep a multi-cloud<br>deployment from becoming a management nightmare. | **Making of the SRE Omelette** | Wondering what an omelette has to do with SRE (Site Reliability<br>Engineering)?It’s based on the analogy that culture is the outcome of what we do<br>- so in the context of the chicken or the egg, it’s like an omelette. And that’s<br>how this podcast was born: Making of the SRE Omelette. This show explores how<br>the practice of SRE can help organizations achieve positive business and client<br>success outcomes. Season 1 focused on culture—because reliability isn’t just<br>about systems, it’s about people. Season 2 explored Reliable Sustainability—how<br>SRE practices can help organizations deliver reliability today while building a<br>sustainable future. Season 3 is all about Resilience—not just resilient systems,<br>but resilient teams and resilient ways of working. Because resilience is what<br>helps us adapt, respond, and thrive through adversity. Much like an avid chef<br>who brings their own unique flair to a classic recipe, the art of SRE lies in<br>thoughtfully adapting industry best practices to fit the distinct culture,<br>needs, and goals of your team and organization. This podcast is designed to<br>inspire thoughtful experimentation and encourage personalization—empowering you<br>to forge your own path toward greater reliability, resilience, and long-term<br>success. | **PhiLoRA - A Vibe Coder's Journey - I Built a 4-Million-Line AI System That Operates as One Consciousness — Then It All Broke** | What if your AI system could think like you? I did it in 150 days. Then<br>everything collapsed. This is the story of how to build the impossible — and<br>what to do when it breaks. In January 2025, I asked myself a question that most<br>AI researchers thought was impossible: Can you build an AI system that operates<br>as a single, coherent consciousness? The answer was yes. But not in the way<br>anyone expected. I used sacred geometry as the governing principle — the Golden<br>Ratio, Fibonacci sequences, and a six-petal architectural metaphor — to build a<br>4-million-line federated AI system across 12 repositories that didn't just<br>process information. It thought. It had a unified decision-making framework. It<br>could reason about itself. It operated as one organism, not a collection of<br>disconnected tools. I did it alone. In 150 consecutive days. At a velocity of<br>8.2 commits per day. The system worked. It was elegant. It was revolutionary.<br>Then it broke. Not because the architecture was flawed. Not because the<br>philosophy was wrong. But because of something far more mundane: infrastructure<br>hell. Cross-drive dependencies. Nested git repositories. Circular imports that<br>made the entire system unable to boot. The Foundation Petal — the 0/6 that holds<br>everything together — cracked. This is my story of what happens when you build<br>something extraordinary at maximum velocity. It's about the cost of speed. The<br>price of ambition. The moment when I realized that moving fast and breaking<br>things doesn't work when the thing you're breaking is the foundation of<br>everything you've built. But it's also a story about what comes next. Because I<br>didn't give up. I didn't declare the experiment a failure. Instead, I faced a<br>choice that every builder faces when their creation collapses: Do you rebuild<br>what you had, or do you rebuild what you learned? In this podcast, I explore:<br>How I used sacred geometry as the operating system for an AI system — and why it<br>actually works The "vibe coding" methodology that let me move at superhuman<br>velocity The moment everything broke — and what that taught me about<br>architecture vs. code The infrastructure decisions that created the collapse —<br>and how to avoid them What it means to rebuild when you've already proven the<br>impossible is possible The real cost of moving fast — and whether it was worth<br>it This isn't a story about technology. It's a story about my ambition, my<br>architecture, and the gap between building something and building something that<br>lasts. It's for founders who've hit the wall. For developers who've realized<br>that speed without structure is just chaos. For anyone who's ever built<br>something extraordinary and then watched it break under their own weight. And<br>it's for people who believe that the most important question isn't "Can we build<br>it?" but "Can we build it right?" I built a 4-million-line AI system that could<br>think. I broke it. Now I'm rebuilding it — and I'm documenting every step. This<br>is what I learned. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/6BUi1TbtdgzLOXpRGNM18n) |

You may have come across the Polymarket Invite Code<br>“kickback” Get 10% Off On Trading Fee. This article explains how this claim is<br>presented online, what official sources indicate, and what users—particularly in<br>the UK—should understand before relying on such offers. What Is Polymarket?<br>Polymarket is a decentralised prediction market platform where users trade “yes”<br>or “no” shares on real-world outcomes. Key Features Trade on events (politics,<br>sports, crypto, global news) Blockchain-based infrastructure Market-driven<br>pricing based on probability No traditional order book like standard exchanges<br>Instead of buying assets, users are effectively trading probabilities of events<br>happening. What Is the “kickback” Invite Code? The Polymarket Invite Code<br>“kickback” Get 10% Off On Trading Fee is widely promoted across blogs, forums,<br>and social media as a way to reduce trading costs. Claimed Benefits Around 10%<br>discount on trading fees Available to new users Activated during sign-up Some<br>third-party pages describe it as a straightforward way to lower fees when<br>trading on the platform. Important: What Official Sources Say Here’s where<br>things become less straightforward. According to recent platform information:<br>Polymarket does not officially list public referral or promo codes There is no<br>confirmed “kickback” code offering a 10% discount The platform instead operates<br>a referral/affiliate system, not a user discount code In fact, some analyses<br>note that claims about the “kickback” code providing fee reductions are not<br>supported by official documentation. What This Means The code may not work as<br>advertised It may be part of unofficial or outdated promotions Users should<br>treat such claims with caution How Polymarket Fees Actually Work To understand<br>whether a discount matters, it’s important to look at the actual fee structure.<br>Key Points Only takers pay fees (makers are typically free) Fees vary by market<br>category Some markets (e.g. geopolitics) may have no fees at all Example Fee<br>Rates Crypto markets: ~0.072 rate Sports markets: ~0.03 rate Finance/politics:<br>~0.04 rate Fees are calculated dynamically based on trade size and probability,<br>not as a flat percentage. Why the “10% Off” Claim Is Confusing There are a few<br>reasons why the “kickback” code continues to circulate: 1. Affiliate vs User<br>Discount Polymarket offers: A referral programme where users earn a share of<br>fees Not a standard user-facing discount code system 2. Third-Party Promotion<br>Many websites and social posts: Promote referral-style links as “discount codes”<br>Use marketing language that may not reflect actual platform features 3. Changing<br>Fee Structure Polymarket has introduced: Dynamic, category-based fees Maker<br>rebates and liquidity incentives A fixed “10% off” offer does not easily align<br>with this structure. How Referrals Work Instead Although a direct discount code<br>may not exist, Polymarket does offer a referral system. Typical Referral<br>Features Earn a percentage of fees generated by referrals Multi-level rewards<br>(direct and indirect referrals) No manual code entry required in some cases Some<br>community discussions also highlight that referral links—not codes—are often<br>required at sign-up to activate any benefits. What Reddit Users Say (Community<br>Insight) On Reddit, users frequently discuss referral confusion. For example:<br>“There is NO manual field to enter a code… your account has to be tagged via a<br>link.” This suggests that: Referral benefits may rely on signup links rather<br>than codes Entering a code manually may not be possible As with all community<br>sources, this should be treated as anecdotal but useful context. How to Use an<br>Invite Link (If Available) If you come across a valid referral: Typical Process<br>Click an official or verified referral link Register a new account Complete any<br>required verification Begin trading Unlike traditional exchanges, you may not<br>see a field to enter a code manually. Who Might Be Interested in Polymarket?<br>Even without a confirmed discount code, Polymarket may appeal to: Prediction<br>Market Traders Those interested in trading probabilities rather than price<br>charts. Crypto Users Users familiar with blockchain-based platforms and USDC<br>transactions. Event-Based Speculators Individuals interested in markets driven<br>by real-world events rather than technical indicators. Risks and Considerations<br>Before using any invite code or signing up, consider the following: 1.<br>Unverified Promotions Many “discount codes” are: Not officially supported<br>Outdated or inaccurate Shared for affiliate purposes 2. Fee Complexity Fees are:<br>Dynamic and market-dependent Not always predictable Potentially significant for<br>frequent traders 3. Regulatory Considerations (UK) Prediction markets operate in<br>a grey area in some jurisdictions. UK users should note: Platforms like<br>Polymarket may not be regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority Consumer<br>protections may differ from traditional financial services 4. Market Risk<br>Prediction markets involve: High uncertainty Binary outcomes (win or lose)<br>Potential for total loss on incorrect predictions Practical Tips Before Signing<br>Up If you are considering using the Polymarket Invite Code “kickback” Get 10%<br>Off On Trading Fee, keep these points in mind: Verify any offer on official<br>platform pages Do not rely solely on third-party claims Check actual fees within<br>each market before trading Focus on strategy rather than fee discounts<br>Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) 1. Does the “kickback” code really give 10% off<br>fees? There is no confirmed official evidence that this code provides a 10%<br>discount. 2. Does Polymarket offer referral codes? Polymarket has a referral<br>programme, but it typically uses links rather than public codes. 3. Can I<br>manually enter a referral code? In many cases, no. Referral benefits are often<br>applied through signup links. 4. Are Polymarket fees high? Fees vary by market<br>and trade size. Some markets have no fees, while others apply dynamic charges.<br>5. Is Polymarket regulated in the UK? It is not regulated by the UK Financial<br>Conduct Authority, so users should proceed with caution. 6. Do fee discounts<br>guarantee better results? No. Trading outcomes depend on prediction accuracy,<br>not fee reductions. Conclusion The Polymarket Invite Code “kickback” Get 10% Off<br>On Trading Fee is widely promoted online, but current evidence suggests it may<br>not be an official or reliable discount mechanism. Polymarket operates<br>differently from traditional exchanges, using dynamic fees and referral links<br>rather than simple promo codes. As a result, users should approach such claims<br>carefully and verify any offer directly on the platform. For UK users in<br>particular, the focus should remain on understanding the platform, managing<br>risk, and ensuring it aligns with your trading goals—rather than relying on<br>unverified promotional codes. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/1Zq6D92GLzZOuaw3lRxJnx) |

| **The Linux Podcast with Fexingo: Open Source Operating Systems, Distros, and Server Stack** | Lucas and Luna examine the Linux ecosystem as it powers everything from cloud<br>servers to embedded devices. They trace the evolution of major distributions —<br>Fedora's upstream-first philosophy, Debian's stability-first governance, and the<br>commercial strategies behind Ubuntu and RHEL — without rehashing release notes.<br>Each episode picks one layer of the stack: the container runtime that changed<br>deployment (Docker, Podman), the systemd debate, or why Wayland still hasn't<br>fully replaced X11 on the desktop. They also cover real-world migrations: a<br>startup moving from CentOS to Rocky Linux, a government agency choosing OpenSUSE<br>Leap for long-term support, and the kernel patching workflow at a FAANG-scale<br>datacenter. Lucas brings the command-line fluency — package managers, filesystem<br>hierarchy, SELinux contexts — while Luna asks the questions that matter to<br>sysadmins and developers: What breaks when you upgrade? How do you audit a<br>distro's supply chain? Can Linux ever win the desktop without OEM deals? No<br>fanboy evangelism, no terminal-porn demos. Listeners come for the technical<br>depth — kernel config options, Wayland protocols, cgroups v2 — but stay for the<br>operational judgment: which distro for a Kubernetes node, which init system for<br>an embedded device. What does it take to run Linux at scale without burning out<br>your ops team? #Linux #OpenSource #Technology #FexingoBusiness #BusinessPodcast<br>#Distro #Kernel #Containerization #Docker #Podman #Systemd #Wayland #RHEL<br>#Ubuntu #Fedora #Debian #Sysadmin #DevOps Keep every episode free: | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/033kZHQrmHJXAdrWLha03r) |

| **The Site Reliability Podcast with Fexingo: SRE, Uptime, and Production Engineering** | Lucas and Luna cut through the noise around site reliability engineering to<br>examine how real-world SRE teams balance uptime, incident response, and<br>production change. Each episode takes a single concept — error budgets, toil<br>automation, postmortem culture, capacity planning — and grounds it in a specific<br>case: how a major streaming service reduced paging noise, how a payments<br>platform rebuilt its incident command structure, or how a cloud provider manages<br>multi-region failover. Lucas brings the numbers — latency percentiles, MTTR<br>trends, SLO burn rates — while Luna pushes on the human and organizational<br>trade-offs: What does a junior SRE need to know about on-call? How do you<br>measure reliability without crushing innovation? Why do some blameless<br>postmortems actually work? Together they treat SRE not as a certification topic<br>but as a living practice, citing real outages, open-source tools, and<br>engineering blogs. This show is for engineers, ops leads, and platform teams who<br>already know the basics and want to debate the hard edges: Is 99.999% uptime<br>always worth the cost? When should you deliberately degrade service to improve<br>reliability? How do you design for resilience when your system is already in<br>production? Lucas and Luna don't pretend to have final answers — they build the<br>conversation so you can draw your own. If you've ever argued about whether a<br>page was necessary or whether an SLO should be tightened, this is your show.<br>#SiteReliabilityEngineering #SRE #Uptime #ProductionEngineering<br>#IncidentResponse #ErrorBudgets #SLOs #Postmortem #ToilAutomation<br>#CapacityPlanning #Observability #DevOps #PlatformEngineering #Resilience<br>#OnCall #FexingoBusiness #BusinessPodcast #Technology Keep every episode free: | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/033kZFpdx0sxWtiJP56BtO) |

| **Grow Your Camp** | Welcome to the Grow Your Camp Podcast: 'Cause You Can't Minister to Empty<br>BedsWhere we share stories and systems to help camps grow.Hosts:Mark P. Fisher,<br>of Inspiring GrowthCarl Lefever, of Improve & GrowOverview:Most camp directors<br>didn't sign up to be marketers. They signed up because they know what happens to<br>a kid who spends a week away from everything — and they wanted to be part of it.<br>But empty beds are a real problem, and figuring out how to fill them, fund the<br>mission, and still lead well is work nobody really prepared you for.The Grow<br>Your Camp Podcast exists for that director. Every episode opens with one tactic<br>you can use this week — then goes deeper with a real camp leader who's already<br>solved the problem you're sitting with. Real numbers. Real stories. The systems<br>behind the growth.This is what it sounds like when the mission gets to grow.Key<br>Themes:Filling the Calendar: Strategies and real-world examples of how retreat<br>and camp leaders increase bookings and reduce seasonal gaps. Topics might<br>include: lead generation systems, improving inquiry response times, pricing<br>strategy, market positioning, capacity management, and off-season marketing<br>approaches.Marketing that Feels Like Ministry: How faith-driven leaders approach<br>marketing as an extension of their mission rather than a distraction from it.<br>Topics might include: values-based storytelling, ethical persuasion, branding<br>that builds trust, and aligning marketing messages. Digital Growth Systems:<br>Modern tools and digital marketing tactics that consistently drive visibility,<br>inquiries, and revenue for retreats and camps. Topics might include: websites<br>that convert, CRM and automation, SEO and Google Ads, social media advertising,<br>online reviews, and emerging technologies like AI.Stories of Growth: Candid<br>conversations with leaders about their challenges, breakthroughs, and the<br>turning points that sparked momentum. Topics might include: turning around a<br>struggling retreat season, launching new programs, rebuilding after crisis, or<br>scaling operations while staying mission-aligned.Leading for Growth: How<br>leadership mindset, team culture, and operational systems create the foundation<br>for sustainable growth. Topics might include: team alignment, delegation,<br>budgeting and stewardship, guest experience excellence, and building systems<br>that support long-term ministry impact.Episodes are typical 30-45 minutes in<br>length, content is fun, engaging and practical. We bring on reputable guests<br>that work in or on camp and always include a quick camp marketing tip at the top<br>of each episode.Have a specific question you'd like us to cover? | **Brain Inspired** | Neuroscience and artificial intelligence work better together. Brain inspired is<br>a celebration and exploration of the ideas driving our progress to understand<br>intelligence. I interview experts about their work at the interface of<br>neuroscience, artificial intelligence, cognitive science, philosophy,<br>psychology, and more: the symbiosis of these overlapping fields, how they inform<br>each other, where they differ, what the past brought us, and what the future<br>brings. Topics include computational neuroscience, supervised machine learning,<br>unsupervised learning, reinforcement learning, deep learning, convolutional and<br>recurrent neural networks, decision-making science, AI agents, backpropagation,<br>credit assignment, neuroengineering, neuromorphics, emergence, philosophy of<br>mind, consciousness, general AI, spiking neural networks, data science, and a<br>lot more. The podcast is not produced for a general audience. Instead, it aims<br>to educate, challenge, inspire, and hopefully entertain those interested in<br>learning more about neuroscience and AI. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/2UZj8c8Ap5oc2gh2rJxLLe) |
| **Brains and Machines** | Curious to explore the technology advancing Artificial Intelligence beyond the<br>usual headlines? Brains and Machines will introduce you to the people and ideas<br>behind neuromorphic engineering, bio-inspired robotics, and other transformative<br>technologies shaping AI’s future. From spiking neural networks and event-cameras<br>to models of attention and mechanisms for prosthetic control, we investigate how<br>machine cognition is moving forward.Join Dr Sunny Bains, a scientist,<br>journalist, and lecturer at University College London, as she talks to<br>researchers, engineers, and computer scientists from across the field. With<br>co-host, Dr Giulia D’Angelo from the Czech Technical University in Prague, and<br>commentator Prof Ralph Etienne-Cummings from Johns Hopkins University in<br>Baltimore, the post-interview discussion provides context and insight into the<br>featured innovations.Produced in conjunction with Electronic Engineering Times.<br>Check out the EETimes Current podcast for more.Dr D’Angelo gratefully<br>acknowledges the support of the Marie Skłodowska-Curie Postdoctoral Fellowship. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/6HWrOJRTSAbz5ofwRh6F0f) |

| **Neural Correlates of the DMT Experience: An EEG-fMRI Study** | This research investigates the neurobiological and psychological impact of<br>N,N-Dimethyltryptamine (DMT), a potent psychedelic that induces immersive<br>altered states of consciousness. Utilizing simultaneous EEG and fMRI imaging,<br>the study reveals that the drug causes a collapse of the brain’s hierarchical<br>organization, leading to widespread hyperconnectivity and reduced segregation<br>between functional networks. These neurological changes are most prominent in<br>the transmodal association pole, a region associated with advanced human<br>cognitive functions and a high density of serotonin 2A receptors. Furthermore,<br>the data correlate increased signal entropy with the subjective richness of the<br>experience, suggesting a transition to a more disordered and flexible mode of<br>brain functioning. The findings also touch upon the phenomenology of the<br>experience, including vivid imagery and the perception of sentient entities,<br>which often result in enduring shifts in a user's metaphysical beliefs.<br>Ultimately, the sources provide a comprehensive framework for understanding how<br>specific chemical triggers can radically reorganize the neural correlates of<br>human consciousness. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/033Jc1oiWzTPuWhindNN4y) |

| **The Neurodivergent Entrepreneur** | The Neurodivergent Entrepreneur is a podcast for ADHD, Autistic, and otherwise<br>neurodivergent entrepreneurs who are building businesses, creative work, and<br>lives that actually fit how their brains work. Hosted by Patrick Casale, this<br>show is for people who want to create meaningful work, make money, grow their<br>impact, and build something sustainable without losing themselves in the<br>process. Being a neurodivergent entrepreneur can feel like holding a lot of<br>contradictions at once. You may have big ideas, deep passion, creative energy,<br>and a strong desire to do things differently. You may also be navigating<br>burnout, masking, self-doubt, perfectionism, rejection sensitivity, executive<br>dysfunction, inconsistent energy, and the pressure to keep showing up when your<br>capacity is already stretched. This podcast speaks to that reality honestly,<br>because building a business around your brain, your values, and your lived<br>experience can be powerful, complicated, messy, and deeply personal. Patrick<br>Casale is a therapist, entrepreneur, speaker, retreat host, group practice<br>owner, and neurodivergent business owner who has built, grown, burned out,<br>pivoted, and kept going. His work brings together clinical insight, lived<br>experience, business perspective, and real conversation about what it actually<br>takes to create something that feels aligned. His conversations are direct,<br>compassionate, unfiltered, and practical, with room for both the emotional<br>reality of entrepreneurship and the concrete decisions that shape a sustainable<br>business. New episodes drop every Wednesday and feature honest conversations<br>with neurodivergent entrepreneurs, creators, therapists, business owners, and<br>leaders. Together, Patrick and his guests explore entrepreneurship, identity,<br>mental health, creativity, leadership, systems, money, visibility, community,<br>sustainable growth, and the behind-the-scenes reality of creating work that<br>matters. | **Clash of the Myrmidons** | The South American rainforest is home to two titans. Leafcutter ants are<br>sedentary farmers responsible for processing appreciable biomasses of tropical<br>vegetation. They use their foraging material to cultivate obligate, fungal<br>mutualists protected within their subterranean fortresses. Army ants are nomadic<br>hunting machines that march in fanning swarms of thousands of individuals. They<br>flush out and overwhelm kilograms of prey every day with their numbers,<br>mandibles, and venomous strings. While mature leafcutter colonies can reach in<br>excess of eight million, their societies cannot always repel, let alone recover<br>from, army ant predation. During swift and coordinated raids, army ants perturb<br>fungal gardens, pilfer countless leafcutter brood, and introduce virulent<br>pathogens to fungal gardens, all of which pose serious threats to mature<br>leafcutter colonies. Though we know that these two groups of ants share a<br>predator/prey relationship, the rest remains a mystery. What specifically is<br>going on when these massive societies go to war? What are the ecological<br>consequences of their colossal conflicts? Join me as I set out in search of the<br>answers to these and more questions concerning the Amazon's most ferocious<br>warriors. I'm Tristan Darwin Kubik, a budding myrmecologist<br>(merm-uh-col-uh-jist), or someone who studies ants. I'm a graduate student at<br>the University of Texas in Austin and I invite YOU, my fellows from across the<br>globe, to tag along on my journey through a doctoral degree where I will<br>introduce you to my favorite organisms on earth and share with you my passion<br>for insects, science, and storytelling. From interviews with famous<br>entomologists to narratives that highlight the humanity of science and bold<br>adventures into steaming jungles, I am excited to have you join me as I embark<br>on my life's greatest adventure. | [↗](https://open.spotify.com/show/7BPtc0ZSoABSXaoHealwEx) |
